title: Very strong light-matter coupling in patterned GaAs heterostructures
authors: David de la Fuente Pico, Johannes Bürger, Antonio Gianfrate, Jesper Levinsen, Meera M. Parish, Daniele Sanvitto, Francesca Maria Marchetti, Dario Ballarini
abstract: The very strong light-matter coupling regime enables the non-perturbative modification of matter properties via light. Using a patterned GaAs/AlGaAs waveguide with twelve wide quantum wells, we demonstrate hybridization of heavy- and light-hole excitons within a single polariton state and show that, at finite magnetic field, the presence of the light-hole exciton suppresses coupling to the heavy-hole Rydberg excitons and unbound scattering states. We develop a fully microscopic theory that accounts for the combined effects of the magnetic field and light-matter coupling on the excitons, providing an accurate description of the experimental results beyond a perturbative coupled-oscillator framework. This identifies quantum well width as a key control parameter for engineering the light-induced hybridization of matter wave functions in polaritons, which, in turn, can play a crucial role in the optical non-linearities.
summary: This paper investigates strong light-matter coupling in patterned GaAs quantum well waveguides, observing the hybridization of heavy- and light-hole excitons into polaritons. By combining advanced magneto-optical measurements with a fully microscopic theoretical model, the authors demonstrate that the quantum well width can precisely engineer these coupled states. This work is crucial for understanding and controlling optical nonlinearities in solid-state quantum emitters.
main_problem: The study aims to demonstrate and theoretically describe the physics of very strong light-matter coupling in patterned GaAs heterostructures, specifically focusing on the hybridization between heavy-hole and light-hole excitons.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated the hybridization of heavy- and light-hole excitons into a single polariton state and developed a microscopic theory that accurately describes this coupling, showing that quantum well width is a key control parameter.
method: The research combines polarization-resolved white-light reflection measurements with the development of a fully microscopic theory that solves the coupled exciton-photon Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The system is a patterned GaAs/AlGaAs waveguide heterostructure containing multiple quantum wells. The theoretical framework uses a Luttinger Hamiltonian for the excitons coupled to the TE waveguide mode, analyzed in a 2D effective model.
key_observables: Polariton energy spectra (measured via reflectance), Rabi couplings ($\Omega_{hh}, \Omega_{lh}$), and the composition (photon/exciton fractions) of the resulting polariton states.
important_parameters: Quantum well width ($d_z$), external magnetic field ($B$ up to $9 	ext{ T}$), and the degree of light-matter coupling strength.
main_assumptions: The analysis often simplifies the full Hamiltonian by using an effective 2D model, neglecting certain off-diagonal Luttinger terms, and assuming TE mode dominance.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate polariton mode anticrossing vs. in-plane wave vector ($\kappa_x$) for varying magnetic fields, and show the exciton/photon composition of the upper polariton states (UP1, UP2) as a function of detuning and magnetic field.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical system and experimental setup to deriving the full Hamiltonian, developing a microscopic 2D model, extracting key parameters (like Rabi couplings) by fitting experimental data, and finally analyzing the energy shifts and state composition in the strong coupling regime.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and quantum optics as it deals with engineered light-matter interactions in solid-state systems, providing quantitative control over exciton-photon hybridization using external fields and material design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Reflectance spectra in the very strong coupling regime as a function of the photon in-plane wave vector κx for increasing values of the magnetic field B. The polariton modes display anticrossing between the TE1,± waveguide modes (white dotted lines) and the the hh and lh exciton energies of states with different polarization, Ehh,ns,± and Elh,1s,± measured in weak coupling (horizontal dashed lines). Solid (orange) lines are the theoretically predicted polariton energies.

FIG. 2. Top panel: Experimental exciton diamagnetic shift (E+ + E−)/2 as a function of magnetic field for the first five states (triangles). Points show theoretical results from the Luttinger Hamiltonian model without adjustable parameters (see text and Ref. [23]), while dashed lines correspond to an effective 2D exciton model with fitted Rydberg energies and reduced masses (see [22]). Bottom panels: Exciton Zeeman splitting E+ −E−for the first five excited states.
FIG. 3. Exciton |Xσ,±|2 = ⟨0| ˆ Xσ,± ˆ X† σ,±|0⟩and photon |γ|2

FIG. 4. Comparison between the experimental (circles) and theoretical (solid lines) energy shift ∆E = E(B) −E(B=0) of the polariton state UP1 at two magnetic fields for the TE1,+ mode. Dashed lines show calculations including light coupling perturbatively (see text).
